Distance-based learning with manydist and tidymodels

Author

Alfonso Iodice D’Enza 

set.seed(123)
rm(list = ls())
devtools::load_all("../")
OK: All competitor packages are already installed.

Competitor packages loaded successfully:
 [1] "proxy"        "parallelDist" "Rfast"        "klaR"         "arules"      
 [6] "cluster"      "FD"           "StatMatch"    "gower"        "clustMixType"
# library(manydist)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(palmerpenguins)

1 Introduction

The manydist package provides a flexible framework for distance-based learning with continuous, categorical, and mixed-type predictors. Its core function mdist() computes mixed-variable dissimilarities under a range of presets and custom specifications, while the MDist class stores both the resulting distance matrix and the associated metadata.

The package also integrates with the tidymodels ecosystem through a recipe step, step_mdist(), a custom distance-based nearest-neighbour model, nearest_neighbor_dist(), a distance-based partitioning around medoids implementation, pam_dist(), and a distance-based spectral clustering implementation, spectral_dist().

This vignette illustrates the main functionalities of the package:

  1. computing mixed-type distances with mdist();
  2. assessing variable influence with lovo_mdist();
  3. comparing diagnostics across methods with compare_lovo_mdist();
  4. creating distance-based representations with step_mdist();
  5. fitting and tuning a KNN model with nearest_neighbor_dist();
  6. fitting a distance-based PAM clustering model with pam_dist();
  7. fitting a distance-based spectral clustering model with spectral_dist().

The focus is on classification of penguin species, but the same ideas apply to other supervised or unsupervised tasks that rely on pairwise distances.

2 Data

We start from the penguins data and remove rows with missing values.

penguins_clean <- palmerpenguins::penguins |>
  drop_na()

penguins_clean |>
  dplyr::count(species)
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie      146
2 Chinstrap    68
3 Gentoo      119

3 Computing mixed-type distances with mdist()

The main entry point of the package is mdist(), which computes a mixed-variable dissimilarity according to a chosen preset or custom specification.

d_obj_all <- mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "u_dep"
)

summary(d_obj_all)
MDist summary
  Preset : u_dep 
  Observations : 333 
  Continuous vars : 5 
  Categorical vars: 2 

Distance matrix statistics:
  Mean : 7.0211 
  SD   : 2.4944 
  Min  : 0.2931 
  Max  : 17.601 
  Diagonal all zeros (block): YES 
  Symmetric (block check)   : YES 
  Triangle viol. rate (block): 0.000 
  NA count:0
  Inf count:0

Commensurable mixed-type distance combining manhattan (continuous) and tvd (categorical) components.

The resulting object stores the distance matrix together with the distance specification.

class(d_obj_all)
[1] "MDist" "R6"   
dim(base::as.matrix(d_obj_all$distance))
[1] 333 333

4 Variable diagnostics with lovo_mdist()

The function lovo_mdist() provides a leave-one-variable-out diagnostic. For each predictor, the function recomputes the distance after removing that variable and compares the resulting structure with the full-distance solution.

lovo_res <- lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "u_dep"
)

summary(lovo_res)
Summary of MDistLOVO
  preset : u_dep 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 
  response used : TRUE 

MAD importance:
  range [1.0000, 1.2957], mean 1.0446

Relative distance:
  range [0.1368, 0.1772], mean 0.1429

MDS congruence:
  range [0.8499, 0.9993], mean 0.9634

Alienation coefficient (AC):
  range [0.0363, 0.5269], mean 0.1999

Top by relative distance:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable          variable_type relative_distance
  <chr>             <chr>                     <dbl>
1 sex               categorical               0.177
2 bill_length_mm    numeric                   0.138
3 body_mass_g       numeric                   0.137
4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                   0.137
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                   0.137

Top by MAD:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable          variable_type mad_importance
  <chr>             <chr>                  <dbl>
1 sex               categorical             1.30
2 bill_length_mm    numeric                 1.01
3 body_mass_g       numeric                 1.00
4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                 1.00
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                 1.00

Top by MDS congruence:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable       variable_type mds_congruence
  <chr>          <chr>                  <dbl>
1 sex            categorical            0.850
2 island         categorical            0.926
3 bill_depth_mm  numeric                0.982
4 body_mass_g    numeric                0.993
5 bill_length_mm numeric                0.996

Top by alienation coefficient:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable       variable_type ac_importance
  <chr>          <chr>                 <dbl>
1 sex            categorical          0.527 
2 island         categorical          0.378 
3 bill_depth_mm  numeric              0.190 
4 body_mass_g    numeric              0.122 
5 bill_length_mm numeric              0.0933

A graphical summary is also available through autoplot().

autoplot(lovo_res, "mad_normalized")

4.1 LOVO with clustering-based diagnostics

In addition to distance- and MDS-based diagnostics, lovo_mdist() can optionally assess how much the clustering solution changes when each variable is removed. This is activated by specifying the number of clusters through cluster_k.

In the next example, we compute leave-one-variable-out diagnostics for the same distance specification as above, and compare the full-data partition with the leave-one-out partitions using both PAM and hierarchical clustering. Agreement is measured through the adjusted Rand index (ARI), while pam_importance and hclust_importance are defined as 1 - ARI, so that larger values indicate stronger influence of the left-out variable.

lovo_res_clust <- lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "u_dep",
  cluster_k = 3
)

summary(lovo_res_clust)
Summary of MDistLOVO
  preset : u_dep 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 
  response used : TRUE 
  cluster_k : 3 
  cluster methods : pam, hclust 
  hclust linkage : average 

MAD importance:
  range [1.0000, 1.2957], mean 1.0446

Relative distance:
  range [0.1368, 0.1772], mean 0.1429

MDS congruence:
  range [0.8499, 0.9993], mean 0.9634

Alienation coefficient (AC):
  range [0.0363, 0.5269], mean 0.1999

ARI vs full PAM partition:
  range [0.1099, 0.9625], mean 0.5389

PAM importance (1 - ARI):
  range [0.0375, 0.8901], mean 0.4611

ARI vs full HCLUST partition:
  range [0.0078, 1.0000], mean 0.6311

HCLUST importance (1 - ARI):
  range [0.0000, 0.9922], mean 0.3689

Top by relative distance:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable          variable_type relative_distance
  <chr>             <chr>                     <dbl>
1 sex               categorical               0.177
2 bill_length_mm    numeric                   0.138
3 body_mass_g       numeric                   0.137
4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                   0.137
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                   0.137

Top by MAD:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable          variable_type mad_importance
  <chr>             <chr>                  <dbl>
1 sex               categorical             1.30
2 bill_length_mm    numeric                 1.01
3 body_mass_g       numeric                 1.00
4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                 1.00
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                 1.00

Top by MDS congruence:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable       variable_type mds_congruence
  <chr>          <chr>                  <dbl>
1 sex            categorical            0.850
2 island         categorical            0.926
3 bill_depth_mm  numeric                0.982
4 body_mass_g    numeric                0.993
5 bill_length_mm numeric                0.996

4 body_mass_g    numeric              0.122 
5 bill_length_mm numeric              0.0933

1 sex               categorical            0.890

4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                0.518
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                0.507

Top by HCLUST importance:
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  variable          variable_type hclust_importance
  <chr>             <chr>                     <dbl>
1 island            categorical               0.992
2 sex               categorical               0.926
3 bill_length_mm    numeric                   0.664
4 bill_depth_mm     numeric                   0    
5 flipper_length_mm numeric                   0

The clustering-based importance measures can be visualized with autoplot() in the same way as the original LOVO diagnostics.

autoplot(lovo_res_clust, metric = "pam_importance")
autoplot(lovo_res_clust, metric = "hclust_importance")

If needed, only one clustering method can be requested.

lovo_res_pam <- lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "u_dep",
  cluster_k = 3,
  cluster_methods = "pam"
)

autoplot(lovo_res_pam, metric = "pam_importance")

5 Comparing distance specifications with compare_lovo_mdist()

The function compare_lovo_mdist() extends the LOVO diagnostic to multiple distance specifications. This makes it possible to compare how variable contributions change across alternative mixed-type distances.

In the example below, we compare:

  • "gower", a standard mixed-type distance;
  • "u_dep", an association-aware distance that enforces commensurability;
  • a custom specification based on Manhattan distance for numeric variables and Hennig–Liao distance for categorical variables.
lovo_cmp <- compare_lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  methods = list(
    gower = list(preset = "gower"),
    u_dep = list(preset = "u_dep"),
    custom = list(
      distance_cont = "manhattan",
      distance_cat  = "HL",
      commensurable = FALSE
    )
  )
)

lovo_cmp
MDistLOVOCompare object
  methods: gower, u_dep, custom 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 
  rows   : 21 

Top variables by MAD within each method:
# A tibble: 9 × 4
  method variable          mad_importance ac_importance
  <fct>  <chr>                      <dbl>         <dbl>
1 gower  island                    0.0730       0.240  
2 gower  sex                       0.0697       0.378  
3 gower  year                      0.0481       0.0501 
4 u_dep  sex                       1.30         0.527  
5 u_dep  bill_length_mm            1.01         0.0933 
6 u_dep  body_mass_g               1.00         0.122  
7 custom body_mass_g             913.           0.416  
8 custom flipper_length_mm        15.9          0.00908
9 custom bill_length_mm            6.27         0.00994
summary(lovo_cmp)
Summary of MDistLOVOCompare
  methods: gower, u_dep, custom 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 

# A tibble: 3 × 10
  method mad_min  mad_max mad_mean   ac_min ac_max ac_mean cc_min cc_max cc_mean
  <fct>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 gower   0.0261   0.0730   0.0431 0.0501    0.378  0.141   0.926  0.999   0.983
2 u_dep   1.000    1.30     1.04   0.0363    0.527  0.200   0.850  0.999   0.963
3 custom  0.883  913.     134.     0.000653  0.416  0.0627  0.910  1.000   0.987
autoplot(lovo_cmp, metric = "mad_normalized")

6 Distance-based KNN in base R

We now move to a simple predictive setting and create a stratified train/test split.

set.seed(123)
peng_split <- initial_split(penguins_clean, strata = species)
peng_train <- training(peng_split)
peng_test  <- testing(peng_split)

dim(peng_train)
[1] 249   8
dim(peng_test)
[1] 84  8

We separate predictors and outcome:

x_train <- peng_train |>
  select(-species)
x_test  <- peng_test |>
  select(-species)
y_train <- peng_train$species
y_test  <- peng_test$species

We compute train-to-test distances with mdist().

test_d_obj <- mdist(
  x = x_train,
  new_data = x_test,
  preset = "u_dep"
)

summary(test_d_obj)
MDist summary
  Preset : u_dep 
  Train-test matrix : 249 train x 84 test
  Continuous vars : 5 
  Categorical vars: 2 

Distance matrix statistics (on preview block):
  Mean : 5.3685 
  SD   : 2.2148 
  Min  : 0.717 
  Max  : 11.2562 
  NA count (block):0
  Inf count (block):0

Commensurable mixed-type distance combining manhattan (continuous) and tvd (categorical) components. 
distance_matrix <- base::as.matrix(test_d_obj$distance)
dim(distance_matrix)
[1]  84 249

These distances can be used directly in a minimal KNN implementation.

n_neighbours <- 5

nn_idx <- apply(distance_matrix, 1, function(d) order(d)[1:n_neighbours])

pred_base <- apply(nn_idx, 2, function(idx) {
  names(which.max(table(y_train[idx])))
})

mean(pred_base == y_test)
[1] 0.9761905

7 Distance-based representations with step_mdist()

To integrate with tidymodels, manydist provides the recipe step step_mdist(), which computes dissimilarities relative to the training set.

  • "distance_to_training" (default): a rectangular test-to-training distance matrix, suited to supervised learners such as KNN;
  • "pairwise": a square within-sample distance matrix, suited to clustering and other unsupervised methods.
peng_rec <- recipe(species ~ ., data = peng_train) |>
  step_mdist(all_predictors(), preset = "u_dep")

peng_rec
Step: mdist
  role:        predictor
  trained:     FALSE

  output:      distance_to_training
  preset:      u_dep
  (arguments handled internally by preset)
prepped_rec <- peng_rec |>
  prep()

As a quick sanity check, the distance matrix obtained through the recipe matches the one computed directly with mdist().

train_dist <- bake(prepped_rec, new_data = peng_train)
prepped_test_d_obj <- bake(prepped_rec, new_data = peng_test)

sum(prepped_test_d_obj |> select(-species) - test_d_obj$distance)
[1] 0

8 Distance-based KNN with nearest_neighbor_dist()

The package also provides a custom parsnip model, nearest_neighbor_dist(), with a "precomputed" engine.

knn_spec <- nearest_neighbor_dist(
  mode = "classification",
  neighbors = 5
) |>
  set_engine("precomputed")

knn_spec
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = 5

Computational engine: precomputed

We can combine the recipe and the model in a workflow.

peng_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(peng_rec) |>
  add_model(knn_spec)

peng_wf
══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preprocessor: Recipe
Model: nearest_neighbor_dist()

── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1 Recipe Step

• step_mdist()

── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = 5

Computational engine: precomputed 
peng_fit <- peng_wf |>
  fit(data = peng_train)

Predictions and performance evaluation are then straightforward.

peng_pred <- predict(peng_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "prob") |>
  bind_cols(
    predict(peng_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "class"),
    peng_test |> select(species)
  )

peng_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 5
  .pred_Adelie .pred_Chinstrap .pred_Gentoo .pred_class species
         <dbl>           <dbl>        <dbl> <fct>       <fct>  
1          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
2          0.8             0.2            0 Adelie      Adelie 
3          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
4          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
5          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
6          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
peng_pred |>
  accuracy(truth = species, estimate = .pred_class)
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric  .estimator .estimate
  <chr>    <chr>          <dbl>
1 accuracy multiclass     0.976

9 Tuning the number of neighbors

Because nearest_neighbor_dist() is registered as a parsnip model, its hyperparameters can be tuned using tune_grid().

knn_tune_spec <- nearest_neighbor_dist(
  mode = "classification",
  neighbors = tune()
) |>
  set_engine("precomputed")

knn_tune_spec
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = tune()

Computational engine: precomputed 
peng_wf_tune <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(peng_rec) |>
  add_model(knn_tune_spec)
set.seed(123)
peng_folds <- vfold_cv(peng_train, v = 5, strata = species)

k_grid <- tibble(neighbors = seq(3, 15, by = 2))
k_grid
# A tibble: 7 × 1
  neighbors
      <dbl>
1         3
2         5
3         7
4         9
5        11
6        13
7        15
peng_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy)

tuned_knn <- tune_grid(
  peng_wf_tune,
  resamples = peng_folds,
  grid = k_grid,
  metrics = peng_metrics
)
collect_metrics(tuned_knn)
# A tibble: 7 × 7
  neighbors .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
      <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1         3 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00774 pre0_mod1_post0
2         5 accuracy multiclass 0.988     5 0.00494 pre0_mod2_post0
3         7 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00403 pre0_mod3_post0
4         9 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod4_post0
5        11 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod5_post0
6        13 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod6_post0
7        15 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod7_post0
show_best(tuned_knn, metric = "accuracy")
# A tibble: 5 × 7
  neighbors .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
      <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1         5 accuracy multiclass 0.988     5 0.00494 pre0_mod2_post0
2         9 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod4_post0
3        11 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod5_post0
4        13 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod6_post0
5        15 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod7_post0
autoplot(tuned_knn)

After selecting the best value of k, the workflow can be finalized and re-fitted on the training set.

best_k <- select_best(tuned_knn, metric = "accuracy")

final_wf <- peng_wf_tune |>
  finalize_workflow(best_k)

final_fit <- final_wf |>
  fit(data = peng_train)

final_pred <- predict(final_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "class") |>
  bind_cols(peng_test |> select(species))

final_pred |>
  accuracy(truth = species, estimate = .pred_class)
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric  .estimator .estimate
  <chr>    <chr>          <dbl>
1 accuracy multiclass     0.976

10 Distance-based clustering with pam_dist() and spectral_dist()

For clustering, step_mdist() is used with output = "pairwise", so that the within-training pairwise dissimilarity matrix is computed and passed to the clustering method.

We first define a recipe that computes the pairwise dissimilarity matrix on the training set.

peng_rec_clust <- recipe(~ ., data = peng_train |> select(-species)) |>
  step_mdist(
    all_predictors(),
    preset = "u_dep",
    output = "pairwise"
  )

peng_rec_clust
Step: mdist
  role:        predictor
  trained:     FALSE

  output:      pairwise
  preset:      u_dep
  (arguments handled internally by preset)

10.1 Distance-based PAM clustering with pam_dist()

We define a PAM clustering specification with three clusters.

pam_spec <- pam_dist(num_clusters = 3)

pam_spec
PAM distance clustering specification
  num_clusters : 3 
  engine       : manydist

The model is then fitted by combining the specification with the recipe and the training predictors.

pam_fit <- generics::fit(
  pam_spec,
  recipe = peng_rec_clust,
  data = peng_train |> select(-species)
)

pam_fit
pam_dist fit
  num_clusters : 3 
  n_obs        : 249 
  medoid idx   : 135, 68, 204

The fitted object returns cluster memberships for the training set.

pam_train_pred <- predict(pam_fit)

pam_train_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 1
  .pred_cluster
  <fct>        
1 1            
2 1            
3 1            
4 2            
5 1            
6 2

Since the true penguin species are known, we can compare the obtained partition with the observed classes using the adjusted Rand index (ARI).

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  as.integer(pam_train_pred$.pred_cluster),
  as.integer(peng_train$species)
)
[1] 0.3604109

For new observations, pam_dist() computes distances from the test set to the training set, extracts the distances to the fitted medoids, and assigns each observation to its nearest medoid.

pam_test_pred <- predict(
  pam_fit,
  new_data = peng_test |> select(-species)
)

pam_test_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 1
  .pred_cluster
  <fct>        
1 2            
2 1            
3 2            
4 1            
5 1            
6 3

The corresponding ARI on the test set is:

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  as.integer(pam_test_pred$.pred_cluster),
  as.integer(peng_test$species)
)
[1] 0.210267

If desired, the distances from each test observation to the medoids can also be extracted directly.

predict(
  pam_fit,
  new_data = peng_test |> select(-species),
  type = "dist"
) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  medoid_1 medoid_2 medoid_3
     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1     7.32     4.34     7.54
2     3.97     6.39     8.86
3     8.85     4.48     8.01
4     4.35     5.81     8.50
5     6.19     6.75     6.22
6     7.46     4.57     4.46

10.2 Distance-based spectral clustering with spectral_dist()

The usage of spectral_dist() parallels that of pam_dist(): the model is fitted on the pairwise training dissimilarity matrix computed by step_mdist(), and predictions for new observations are obtained by extending the spectral embedding to the test set.

We define a spectral clustering specification with three clusters.

spectral_spec <- spectral_dist(
  num_clusters = 3
)

spectral_spec
Spectral distance clustering specification
  num_clusters : 3 
  sigma        : NULL (median heuristic) 
  nstart       : 50 
  engine       : manydist

The model is fitted in the same way as pam_dist().

spectral_fit <- generics::fit(
  spectral_spec,
  recipe = peng_rec_clust,
  data = peng_train |> select(-species)
)

spectral_fit
spectral_dist fit
  num_clusters : 3 
  n_obs        : 249 
  sigma        : 7.052765 
  nstart       : 50

The fitted object returns cluster memberships for the training set.

spectral_train_pred <- predict(spectral_fit)

spectral_train_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 1
  .pred_cluster
  <fct>        
1 1            
2 1            
3 1            
4 2            
5 1            
6 2

As for PAM, the resulting partition can be compared with the observed penguin species using the adjusted Rand index.

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  as.integer(spectral_train_pred$.pred_cluster),
  as.integer(peng_train$species)
)
[1] 0.12579

Predictions for new observations are obtained through the stored distance preprocessor and an out-of-sample extension of the spectral embedding.

spectral_test_pred <- predict(
  spectral_fit,
  new_data = peng_test |> select(-species)
)

spectral_test_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 1
  .pred_cluster
  <fct>        
1 2            
2 1            
3 2            
4 1            
5 1            
6 3

The corresponding ARI on the test set is:

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  as.integer(spectral_test_pred$.pred_cluster),
  as.integer(peng_test$species)
)
[1] 0.05340607

If desired, the spectral embedding coordinates can also be extracted directly.

predict(spectral_fit, type = "embed") |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
   dim_1  dim_2  dim_3
   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 -0.495 -0.631 -0.597
2 -0.516 -0.472 -0.714
3 -0.568 -0.627 -0.533
4 -0.496  0.593 -0.635
5 -0.506 -0.644 -0.573
6 -0.448  0.516 -0.730
predict(
  spectral_fit,
  new_data = peng_test |> select(-species),
  type = "embed"
) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
   dim_1  dim_2  dim_3
   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 -0.610  0.378 -0.696
2 -0.577 -0.624 -0.527
3 -0.476  0.479 -0.737
4 -0.566 -0.601 -0.564
5 -0.795 -0.587  0.153
6 -0.826  0.479  0.297

10.3 Sanity check: direct distance matrix versus recipe-based clustering

Before illustrating the two clustering interfaces, we verify that the recipe-based workflow is consistent with the direct use of mdist().

We first compute the training dissimilarity matrix directly with mdist().

d_train_obj <- mdist(
  x = peng_train |> select(-species),
  preset = "u_dep"
)

D_train_direct <- as.matrix(d_train_obj$distance)
dim(D_train_direct)
[1] 249 249

We then fit PAM directly on this matrix.

pam_direct <- cluster::pam(
  x = stats::as.dist(D_train_direct),
  k = 3,
  diss = TRUE
)

pam_direct_clust <- pam_direct$clustering
head(pam_direct_clust)
1 2 3 4 5 6 
1 1 1 2 1 2

Now we fit the recipe-based pam_dist() model.

pam_spec <- pam_dist(num_clusters = 3)

pam_fit <- generics::fit(
  pam_spec,
  recipe = peng_rec_clust,
  data = peng_train |> select(-species)
)

pam_recipe_clust <- predict(pam_fit)$.pred_cluster |>
  as.integer()

head(pam_recipe_clust)
[1] 1 1 1 2 1 2

The two PAM partitions coincide up to label permutation. We check this through the adjusted Rand index.

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  pam_direct_clust,
  pam_recipe_clust
)
[1] 1

We can do the same for spectral clustering. First, fit the model directly from the distance matrix.

spectral_direct_spec <- spectral_dist(num_clusters = 3)

spectral_direct_fit <- generics::fit(
  spectral_direct_spec,
  x = d_train_obj
)

spectral_direct_clust <- predict(spectral_direct_fit)$.pred_cluster |>
  as.integer()

head(spectral_direct_clust)
[1] 3 3 3 2 3 2

For a stricter direct check based only on the distance matrix, we can also reconstruct the spectral solution from D_train_direct.

aff <- .affinity_from_dist(D_train_direct, sigma = NULL)
A <- aff$A

d <- rowSums(A)
Dhalf_inv <- diag(ifelse(d > 0, 1 / sqrt(d), 0), nrow(A), ncol(A))
S <- Dhalf_inv %*% A %*% Dhalf_inv

ev <- eigen(S, symmetric = TRUE)
U <- ev$vectors[, seq_len(3), drop = FALSE]
U_norm <- .row_normalize(U)

set.seed(123)
spectral_direct_matrix <- stats::kmeans(U_norm, centers = 3, nstart = 50)$cluster

head(spectral_direct_matrix)
[1] 3 3 3 1 3 1

Now fit the recipe-based spectral_dist() model.

spectral_spec <- spectral_dist(num_clusters = 3)

spectral_fit <- generics::fit(
  spectral_spec,
  recipe = peng_rec_clust,
  data = peng_train |> select(-species)
)

spectral_recipe_clust <- predict(spectral_fit)$.pred_cluster |>
  as.integer()

head(spectral_recipe_clust)
[1] 3 3 3 2 3 2

Again, we compare the resulting partitions via the adjusted Rand index.

mclust::adjustedRandIndex(
  spectral_direct_matrix,
  spectral_recipe_clust
)
[1] 1

An ARI equal to 1 confirms that the clustering obtained through the recipe-based pipeline matches the one obtained by first computing the distance matrix with mdist() and then applying the clustering method directly.

10.4 Consistency checks on continuous data

In the special case of purely continuous predictors, the downstream interfaces provided by manydist should be consistent with the corresponding standard methods applied directly to the original data matrix under Euclidean geometry. For simplicity, we illustrate these checks on continuous data only, since Euclidean distance provides a natural and unambiguous reference in that setting. We then verify this for distance computation, nearest neighbours, PAM clustering, and spectral clustering. We start by restricting the penguin data to continuous predictors only.

library(class)
library(cluster)
library(kernlab)
library(mclust)

peng_train_cont <- peng_train |>
  select(species, where(is.numeric))

peng_test_cont <- peng_test |>
  select(species, where(is.numeric))

x_train_cont <- peng_train_cont |> select(-species)
x_test_cont  <- peng_test_cont  |> select(-species)
y_train_cont <- peng_train_cont$species
y_test_cont  <- peng_test_cont$species

X_train <- as.matrix(x_train_cont)
X_test  <- as.matrix(x_test_cont)

k_knn   <- 5
k_clust <- 3

10.4.1 Euclidean distances: direct computation versus mdist()

We first verify that mdist() reproduces ordinary Euclidean distances on continuous data when used with Euclidean distance, no scaling, and no commensurability correction.

D_train_eucl <- as.matrix(stats::dist(X_train))

.euclid_block <- function(x_new, x_ref) {
  x_new <- as.matrix(x_new)
  x_ref <- as.matrix(x_ref)

  n_new <- nrow(x_new)
  all_x <- rbind(x_new, x_ref)
  D_all <- as.matrix(stats::dist(all_x))

  D_all[seq_len(n_new), n_new + seq_len(nrow(x_ref)), drop = FALSE]
}

D_test_train_eucl <- .euclid_block(X_test, X_train)

d_train_mdist <- mdist(
  x = x_train_cont,
  distance_cont = "euclidean",
  scaling_cont = "none",
  commensurable = FALSE
)

D_train_mdist <- as.matrix(d_train_mdist$distance)

d_test_mdist <- mdist(
  x = x_train_cont,
  new_data = x_test_cont,
  distance_cont = "euclidean",
  scaling_cont = "none",
  commensurable = FALSE
)

D_test_train_mdist <- as.matrix(d_test_mdist$distance)

The maximum absolute difference is negligible on the training set and zero on the test-to-training distance block.

distance_check <- tibble::tibble(
  dataset = c("train", "test"),
  check = c(
    "base Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff",
    "test-to-train Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff"
  ),
  value = c(
    max(abs(D_train_eucl - D_train_mdist)),
    max(abs(D_test_train_eucl - D_test_train_mdist))
  )
)

distance_check
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  dataset check                                              value
  <chr>   <chr>                                              <dbl>
1 train   base Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff          7.11e-15
2 test    test-to-train Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff 1.78e-15

10.4.2 KNN: direct Euclidean KNN versus nearest_neighbor_dist()

We now compare standard KNN on the original continuous predictors with the manydist interface based on Euclidean dissimilarities.

knn_direct_pred <- class::knn(
  train = X_train,
  test  = X_test,
  cl    = y_train_cont,
  k     = k_knn
)

The corresponding manydist pipeline uses step_mdist() to compute the Euclidean test-to-training distance representation.

rec_knn_cont <- recipe(species ~ ., data = peng_train_cont) |>
  step_mdist(
    all_predictors(),
    distance_cont = "euclidean",
    scaling_cont = "none",
    commensurable = FALSE
  )

knn_spec_cont <- nearest_neighbor_dist(
  mode = "classification",
  neighbors = k_knn
) |>
  set_engine("precomputed")

wf_knn_cont <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_knn_cont) |>
  add_model(knn_spec_cont)

knn_fit_cont <- fit(wf_knn_cont, data = peng_train_cont)

knn_manydist_pred <- predict(
  knn_fit_cont,
  new_data = peng_test_cont,
  type = "class"
)$.pred_class

We compare the direct and manydist-based predictions through exact agreement and report the manydist classification accuracy on the test set.

knn_check <- tibble::tibble(
  dataset = c("test", "test"),
  check = c(
    "direct vs manydist exact agreement",
    "manydist accuracy"
  ),
  value = c(
    mean(knn_direct_pred == knn_manydist_pred),
    yardstick::accuracy_vec(y_test_cont, knn_manydist_pred)
  )
)

knn_check
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  dataset check                              value
  <chr>   <chr>                              <dbl>
1 test    direct vs manydist exact agreement 0.988
2 test    manydist accuracy                  0.810

10.4.3 PAM: direct Euclidean PAM versus pam_dist()

For PAM clustering, the direct reference is cluster::pam() applied to the original continuous feature matrix with Euclidean metric.

pam_direct_fit <- cluster::pam(
  x = X_train,
  k = k_clust,
  metric = "euclidean"
)

pam_direct_train <- pam_direct_fit$clustering

The corresponding manydist interface is based on a pairwise Euclidean dissimilarity matrix produced through step_mdist(..., output = "pairwise").

rec_clust_cont <- recipe(~ ., data = x_train_cont) |>
  step_mdist(
    all_predictors(),
    distance_cont = "euclidean",
    scaling_cont = "none",
    commensurable = FALSE,
    output = "pairwise"
  )

pam_spec_cont <- pam_dist(num_clusters = k_clust)

pam_manydist_fit <- generics::fit(
  pam_spec_cont,
  recipe = rec_clust_cont,
  data = x_train_cont
)

pam_manydist_train <- predict(pam_manydist_fit)$.pred_cluster |>
  as.integer()

We compare the two training partitions via the adjusted Rand index.

pam_check <- tibble::tibble(
  dataset = "train",
  check = "direct vs manydist partition ARI",
  value = mclust::adjustedRandIndex(pam_direct_train, pam_manydist_train)
)

pam_check
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  dataset check                            value
  <chr>   <chr>                            <dbl>
1 train   direct vs manydist partition ARI     1

10.4.4 Spectral clustering: direct Gaussian-kernel clustering versus spectral_dist()

For spectral clustering, we compare spectral_dist() with a direct spectral clustering benchmark on the original continuous data using kernlab::specc() and a Gaussian kernel. The Gaussian bandwidth is matched to the median Euclidean distance used by the manydist implementation.

sigma_sc <- stats::median(D_train_eucl[upper.tri(D_train_eucl)])
gamma_kernlab <- 1 / (2 * sigma_sc^2)

set.seed(123)
spectral_direct_train <- kernlab::specc(
  x = X_train,
  centers = k_clust,
  kernel = "rbfdot",
  kpar = list(sigma = gamma_kernlab)
)

spectral_direct_train <- as.integer(spectral_direct_train)

The corresponding manydist interface uses the same number of clusters and bandwidth, and is fitted on the pairwise Euclidean dissimilarity matrix produced by step_mdist().

spectral_spec_cont <- spectral_dist(
  num_clusters = k_clust,
  sigma = sigma_sc
)

set.seed(123)
spectral_manydist_fit <- generics::fit(
  spectral_spec_cont,
  recipe = rec_clust_cont,
  data = x_train_cont
)

spectral_manydist_train <- predict(spectral_manydist_fit)$.pred_cluster |>
  as.integer()

We again compare the two training partitions via the adjusted Rand index.

spectral_check <- tibble::tibble(
  dataset = "train",
  check = "direct vs manydist partition ARI",
  value = mclust::adjustedRandIndex(spectral_direct_train, spectral_manydist_train)
)

spectral_check
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  dataset check                            value
  <chr>   <chr>                            <dbl>
1 train   direct vs manydist partition ARI     1

10.4.5 Summary of consistency checks

We finally collect the results into a single summary table.

sanity_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  method = c(
    "Distance matrix",
    "Distance matrix",
    "KNN",
    "KNN",
    "PAM",
    "Spectral"
  ),
  dataset = c(
    "train",
    "test",
    "test",
    "test",
    "train",
    "train"
  ),
  check = c(
    "base Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff",
    "test-to-train Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff",
    "direct vs manydist exact agreement",
    "manydist accuracy",
    "direct vs manydist partition ARI",
    "direct vs manydist partition ARI"
  ),
  value = c(
    distance_check$value[1],
    distance_check$value[2],
    knn_check$value[1],
    knn_check$value[2],
    pam_check$value,
    spectral_check$value
  )
)

sanity_summary
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  method          dataset check                                            value
  <chr>           <chr>   <chr>                                            <dbl>
1 Distance matrix train   base Euclidean vs mdist() max abs diff        7.11e-15
2 Distance matrix test    test-to-train Euclidean vs mdist() max abs d… 1.78e-15
3 KNN             test    direct vs manydist exact agreement            9.88e- 1
4 KNN             test    manydist accuracy                             8.10e- 1
5 PAM             train   direct vs manydist partition ARI              1   e+ 0
6 Spectral        train   direct vs manydist partition ARI              1   e+ 0

These results confirm that, on purely continuous data, mdist() reproduces Euclidean geometry essentially exactly, and that the downstream manydist interfaces are consistent with the corresponding standard methods applied directly to the original data matrix.

11 Summary

In this vignette we showed how manydist can be used for distance-based learning on mixed data through:

  • mixed-type distance computation with mdist();
  • variable diagnostics with lovo_mdist();
  • method comparison with compare_lovo_mdist();
  • recipe-based representations with step_mdist();
  • distance-based KNN modeling and tuning with nearest_neighbor_dist();
  • distance-based PAM clustering with pam_dist();
  • distance-based spectral clustering with spectral_dist().
